metadata.title: Supplemental Material for Spirituality and Meaning-Making Across Contexts: Structural Topic Modeling of the Fetzer Spirituality Study in the United States

abstract: Given the significant shifts in religious affiliation and spiritual seeking over the last 20 years, a rigorous and updated understanding of how spirituality functions are essential to understand the impact of these changes on individuals and society. Drawing on an integrated view of spirituality informed by relational developmental systems (RDS) and meaning-making approaches, in the present study, we sought to identify commonalities within varied expressions of spirituality described within interviews of a religiously and spiritually diverse sample of adults. Structural topic modeling (STM) was applied to infer the latent themes inherent in the indepth narratives of 16 participants from the Fetzer Institute's (2020) Study of Spirituality in the United States. Following STM procedures, 1,298 terms were analyzed, resulting in a three-topic model as the best fit. We applied an RDS and contextual lens to name the topics: Individual, Transcendent, and Social, reflecting different systems in which spirituality is experienced. We found evidence that the process of making meaning is common to how people experience, understand, and then live out their spirituality. Our findings suggest that people experience spirituality when ascribing meaning and pursuing purposeful behaviors based on their transcendent beliefs and experiences at the individual, social, and transcendent levels. Our study sheds light on the importance and viability of considering diverse beliefs about and experiences of transcendence and provides unified and contrasting expressions of spirituality as it is lived out in the United States in the early 21st century through vivid descriptions from diverse lived experiences.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: As public trends in religious and spiritual affiliations evolve (see Pew Research Center, 2021), and research on religiousness and spirituality (R/S) proliferates, the Psychology of Religion and Spirituality faces challenges to both expand its understanding of diverse forms of R/S and deepen the field's theoretical and methodological rigor (Ammerman, 2013;Paloutzian & Park, 2021;Pargament et al., 2013). More nuanced and integrated perspectives of spirituality require further empirical research to continue to clarify the psychological processes involved in the various forms of R/S and how R/S may be contributing to individual well-being and to the collective good. With the intent to further understand the processes at play in diverse forms of R/S, in the present study, we aimed to identify commonalities among varied expressions of spirituality described within the interview transcripts of a sample of adults with diverse R/S affiliations, experiences, and beliefs. Specifically, we applied an unsupervised form of "machine-learning" semantic analysis, structural topic modeling (STM, Roberts et al., 2016), to interviews conducted through Fetzer Institute's (2020) "Study of Spirituality in the United States" in order to identify latent themes and explore their varied expressions.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
sections.header_raw: Current R/S Landscape Within the United States
sections.header_norm: current r s landscape within the united states
paragraphs: Research reveals that people in the United States experience R/S in increasingly diverse ways. In 2007, 78.4% of Americans identified as Christian (Pew Research Center, 2015), while 15 years later only 63% of all adults identified with some religious affiliation (Pew Research Center, 2021). Twenty-seven percent of adults currently consider themselves spiritual but not religious. Forty-eight percent of adults say they are both religious and spiritual, and only 6% say they are religious but not spiritual (Pew Research Center, 2021). Other categories include those who consider themselves to be "unaffiliated" or religious "Nones" (28%). Within the "unaffiliated groups" within the United States, Pew Research Center (2021) found that 5% were Atheists, 6% stated they are Agnostic, and 18% identified as nothing in particular. Trends, such as the rise of the "Nones" who self-identify as neither religious nor spiritual and the "Dones" who report leaving religion, require a more thorough psychological understanding of R/S that allows for nomothetic and idiosyncratic findings.

Given the trends in the United States about issues of faith and spirituality, the psychological assumption that all people are like all other people and like some other people and that each person is like no other person (Kluckholm & Murray, 1948), R/S (Abo-Zena & King, 2021;King et al., 2021), and the lack of consensus, with the many theories focusing on specific components of R/S (e.g., beliefs, salience), we sought a broad theoretical frame and method that would allow us to identify common psychological processes involved in the various forms of R/S with minimal bias.

paragraphs: Because the psychological study of R/S evolved across different subdisciplines, conceptual and theoretical cohesion remain an issue. In order to bridge gaps across some of the silos and frame our interpretation, we draw on major theories from often separated subdisciplines: Developmental Psychology and Psychology of Religion and Spirituality. Specifically, relational developmental systems (RDS) metatheory (King et al., 2021;Lerner, 2018;Overton, 2015) and meaning-making approaches (Immordino-Yang, 2016;Park, 2010;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021) guide our understanding of spirituality.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: RDS metatheories provide comprehensive frameworks for understanding human development, highlighting that all organisms are embodied and embedded and emphasizing that development occurs through bidirectional relationships across different levels of developing systems (Lerner, 2018;Overton, 2015). RDS provides a helpful lens for understanding R/S development because it encourages investigating the many psychological capacities involved in R/S and how they can develop in complex and diverse ways within and between people and across various systems (Davis et al., 2023;Hardy & King, 2019;King et al., 2021). This includes macrosystemic influences such as an individual's beliefs about and their perceptions of the sacred or transcendent.

From an RDS perspective, spiritual development occurs through ongoing interactions between individuals and their perceptions of a source of transcendence or immanence (i.e., God, higher power). Experiences of transcendence inspire meaning and fidelity to beliefs and worldviews that motivate valued-aligned behaviors, which in turn perpetuate the pursuit of transcendence (King & Boyatzis, 2015, King et al., 2014, 2020, 2021;Lerner et al., 2003). This formulation, referred to as reciprocating spirituality, emphasizes the bidirectional relationship between a person's cognitiveaffective experience and social-behavioral response to transcendence (Balswick et al., 2016;King & Boyatzis, 2015). In this literature, spirituality involves the psychological capacities necessary for transcending the self, meaning-making, fidelity, and the pursuit of purposeful behaviors. RDS emphasizes that individual-level cognitive, affective, and sensory processes are shaped by and shape social-level and transcendent-level contexts.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Meaning-making approaches offer more detailed insights into the ways in which people respond to their perceptions or experiences of transcendence or immanence-whether supernatural, sacred, immanent, or otherwise (King et al., 2023;Park, 2010;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). The process by which people derive meaning and determine significance about their lives informs the values, goals, identities, and relationships that guide and direct behaviors aligned with their spirituality (Furrow et al., 2004;Immordino-Yang, 2016;King et al., 2014King et al., , 2023;;Park, 2010;Park & Van Tongeren, 2023;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Park's (2010) approach is most prominent in the Psychology of Religion and Spirituality and identifies that R/S beliefs often inform an individual's global meaning system. Park's model conveys how people assimilate adversity into their global meaning system or accommodate their meaning systems to cope with trying situations. Park's research shows that people evaluate and respond to information from micro to macro levels to adjust their meaning systems by drawing on their R/S beliefs, communities, and practices for making sense out of loss and struggle (Paloutzian & Park, 2021;Park, 2010). These ideas are aligned with recent descriptions of positive R/S development, which assert that because the mind is a self-organizing system, people naturally seek coherence and consequently develop "patterns of thinking, feeling, relating, and behaving" that comprise R/S habits (e.g., reflection, virtue) based on their spiritual search for sacred meaning that might occur through prayer, worship, or service (Davis et al., 2023, p. 285).

Recent research within the fields of education and Developmental Psychology elaborates on the meaning-making process and provides insight into how spirituality develops across individual, social, and macro (e.g., perceived transcendent) systems. Within this literature, meaning-making is broadly recognized as the process of identifying and internalizing beliefs that have enduring salience to the individual through informing narrative identity, values, and behaviors (Immordino-Yang, 2016). Research on meaning-making provides a neuroscientific perspective on how beliefs are incorporated into narrative identity when abstract reflection cooccurs with transcendent or moral emotions and highlights the seminal role of caring relationships in this process (Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Thus, R/S are especially conducive to facilitating meaning-making because they are rife with opportunities to consider transcendent beliefs and experience transcendent emotions through practices (e.g., prayer, worship) and/or caring interpersonal relationships (Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Research demonstrates that thoughts and beliefs become meaningful when accompanied by transcendent emotions such as awe, gratitude, or elevation, which often occurs in the context of caring relationships. When beliefs invoke these emotions, they are ascribed motivational properties and become values (Merrill & Fivush, 2016) incorporated into one's narrative identity (King et al., 2020(King et al., , 2023)). Because apprehending or perceiving transcendence requires some of the same cognitive and affective processes involved in meaning-making (Immordino-Yang, 2016), experiences of transcendence and meaning-making often cooccur (King et al., 2023;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). The ephemeral qualities of boundlessness and ultimacy associated with various forms of perceptions of transcendence involve abstract thinking and the experience of transcendent emotions (Van Cappellen et al., 2023;Van Cappellen & Saroglou, 2012;Yaden et al., 2017). Meaning-making helps researchers operationalize at least part of human transformation attributed to spirituality.

In summary, both RDS and meaning-making approaches highlight that people derive ultimate meaning for their lives through transcendence. RDS approaches, like reciprocating spirituality, emphasize the various contexts or systems in which spiritual development occurs and that spiritual development involves reciprocity in which a person This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

experiences and responds to transcendence. Meaning-making approaches shed more light on how beliefs about transcendence become salient and are incorporated into identity, resulting in fidelity that sustains value-aligned behaviors. Both approaches recognize that although spirituality may occur within or outside a religious tradition, it involves a connection to what is perceived as sacred, ultimate, and/or beyond the self (King et al., 2021;Pargament et al., 2013;Park, 2010;Yaden et al., 2021). Consequently, people report different referents of transcendence or immanence that may be theistic, naturalistic, or humanistic (Ammerman, 2013;Callaway et al., 2020;Steensland et al., 2018) and that are experienced as sources of ultimacy, boundless, and exist beyond our immediate and familiar reality (Pargament et al., 2013). In the present study, we aimed to explore these varied understandings and experiences of spirituality. Although we had theoretical leanings toward a reciprocating spirituality frame that acknowledges the breadth of psychological capacities involved in how people experience and respond to various beliefs about transcendence and make meaning, we sought a method that would allow us to broadly consider commonalities among diverse narratives about spirituality with minimal human and theoretical bias.

Research on meaning-making provides a neuroscientific perspective on how beliefs are incorporated into narrative identity when abstract reflection cooccurs with transcendent or moral emotions and highlights the seminal role of caring relationships in this process (Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Thus, R/S are especially conducive to facilitating meaning-making because they are rife with opportunities to consider transcendent beliefs and experience transcendent emotions through practices (e.g., prayer, worship) and/or caring interpersonal relationships (Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Research demonstrates that thoughts and beliefs become meaningful when accompanied by transcendent emotions such as awe, gratitude, or elevation, which often occurs in the context of caring relationships. When beliefs invoke these emotions, they are ascribed motivational properties and become values (Merrill & Fivush, 2016) incorporated into one's narrative identity (King et al., 2020(King et al., , 2023)). Because apprehending or perceiving transcendence requires some of the same cognitive and affective processes involved in meaning-making (Immordino-Yang, 2016), experiences of transcendence and meaning-making often cooccur (King et al., 2023;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). The ephemeral qualities of boundlessness and ultimacy associated with various forms of perceptions of transcendence involve abstract thinking and the experience of transcendent emotions (Van Cappellen et al., 2023;Van Cappellen & Saroglou, 2012;Yaden et al., 2017). Meaning-making helps researchers operationalize at least part of human transformation attributed to spirituality.

In summary, both RDS and meaning-making approaches highlight that people derive ultimate meaning for their lives through transcendence. RDS approaches, like reciprocating spirituality, emphasize the various contexts or systems in which spiritual development occurs and that spiritual development involves reciprocity in which a person This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

experiences and responds to transcendence. Meaning-making approaches shed more light on how beliefs about transcendence become salient and are incorporated into identity, resulting in fidelity that sustains value-aligned behaviors. Both approaches recognize that although spirituality may occur within or outside a religious tradition, it involves a connection to what is perceived as sacred, ultimate, and/or beyond the self (King et al., 2021;Pargament et al., 2013;Park, 2010;Yaden et al., 2021). Consequently, people report different referents of transcendence or immanence that may be theistic, naturalistic, or humanistic (Ammerman, 2013;Callaway et al., 2020;Steensland et al., 2018) and that are experienced as sources of ultimacy, boundless, and exist beyond our immediate and familiar reality (Pargament et al., 2013). In the present study, we aimed to explore these varied understandings and experiences of spirituality. Although we had theoretical leanings toward a reciprocating spirituality frame that acknowledges the breadth of psychological capacities involved in how people experience and respond to various beliefs about transcendence and make meaning, we sought a method that would allow us to broadly consider commonalities among diverse narratives about spirituality with minimal human and theoretical bias.
sections.preview_head: Meaning-making approaches offer more detailed insights into the ways in which people respond to their perceptions or experiences of transcendence or immanence-whether supernatural, sacred, immanent, or otherwise (King et al., 2023;Park, 2010;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). The process by which people derive meaning and determine significance about their lives informs the values, goals, identities, and relationships that guide and direct behaviors aligned with their spirituality (Furrow et al., 2004;Immordino-Yang, 2016;King et al., 2014King et al., , 2023;;Park, 2010;Park & Van Tongeren, 2023;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Park's (2010) approach is most prominent in the Psychology of Religion and Spirituality and identifies that R/S beliefs often inform an individual's global meaning system.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Given our goal to further psychological perspectives of R/S and to investigate how people with less conventional religious affiliations understand and experience spirituality, within this broad framework of reciprocating spirituality, we sought a data analytic method to test for commonalities across a data set of interviews providing deep descriptions of spirituality from a religiously and spiritually diverse sample. Specifically, we used STM to identify latent topics or themes across such a data set and to minimize the bias of human coding analysis. Unlike guided analytical approaches like dictionary methods (e.g., Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count), in which text data are assigned to predefined classes utilizing the words in the text, unsupervised learning methods, like STM, bundle data into unknown categories, while simultaneously inferring and discovering latent categories (or topics) based on word cooccurrences. STM is considered a mixed-membership model because pieces of text data can belong to multiple latent classes at the same time (Airoldi et al., 2014). In addition to identifying themes common across the data, STM identifies the sources of data (in our case participant interviews) in which a specific topic is most prevalent. The identification of interviews that best represent a topic provides examples of unique expressions of the themes and offers a broader narrative context within the interview to understand the meaning of the themes. Also, STM uses covariates to explain the variation of topic proportions across the interviews (Roberts et al., 2016). Previous research demonstrated that STM is effective in analyzing data sets involving R/S (Kim et al., 2020). Although STM is an unsupervised semantic analytic method, the naming of the topics is informed by existing theory, much like the naming and interpretation of factors in factor analysis. Consequently, we framed our analysis through two dominant theories of spirituality: RDS and meaning-making approaches.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: In the present study, we strove to respond to the admonition in a recent special edition of Psychology of Religion and Spirituality (Paloutzian & Park, 2021) asking researchers to regroup and rethink their conceptual frameworks in order to push toward a higher standard of rigor within Psychology of Religion and Spirituality. We designed our research around an unbiased exploration of common themes and the identification of unique expressions of descriptions of people's varied understanding and experiences of spirituality, regardless of religious affiliation. We employed STM, with 16 in-depth interviews from Fetzer Institute's (2020) Spirituality Study in the United States while framing our understanding of spirituality around a synthesis of RDS and meaning-making approaches.

sections.header_norm: r s measures and interview protocol
paragraphs: Hattaway Communications developed the interview protocol in partnership with the Fetzer Institute and their Spirituality Study in the United States advisors, which included a group of nationally recognized experts from various disciplines that study R/S. The interviews used a life-history approach and included specific This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. questions about spirituality, religion, and civic action. Fetzer's aim was to: (a) explore people's stories and narratives about their lives in order to understand growth, transformation, and spiritual/religious development; (b) explore how people collectively understand, define, experience, and embody religiousness and spirituality; and c) elicit language that captured experiences and meanings that help to inductively define spirituality (See Supplemental File A, for full interview protocol). In addition, participants were asked to rate their levels of religiosity and spirituality.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: The sample was generated through the University of Chicago's National Opinion Research Center's (NORC) AmeriSpeak Panel. NORC distributed a web-based screener survey to its panel asking questions about their R/S affiliation, religious attendance, and to what extent they consider themselves religious or spiritual. Of the initial 200 respondents, Fetzer selected and contacted 90 participants to represent diverse race and ethnicity, age, gender, religion, geographical region, political ideology, educational level, and socioeconomic status. Of the 90 contacted, 40 demonstrated interest in participation. Ultimately, 26, hour-long interviews were conducted via telephone between October and November 2018. Eight of the interviews were lost in a data storage error by the data collectors. Our analysis includes the remaining 16 interviews.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Prior to STM analysis, the interview transcripts were preprocessed (e.g., removing punctuation and stop words). The technical details of the preprocessing as well as the programs for preprocessing and STM analysis are described in online Supplemental File B.

Given STM's capacity to incorporate covariates into its model, it would be informative if the two covariates of religiosity and spirituality can predict the topic proportions in the interviews (Roberts et al., 2016). For example, what are the favorite (or nonfavorite) topics for the highly spiritual but not religious or vice versa? The two covariates were dichotomized prior to data analysis, combining "very" and "moderate" into one category and "slightly" and "not at all" into the other (see Table 1). Because one category of religiosity had only two people, thus lacking enough variance, this covariate was not considered, and the STM models only included spirituality as the covariate.

STM is analogous to exploratory factor analysis (EFA) in many ways including how to fit and identify models, how to select the number of latent dimensions (called topics in STM and factors in EFA), and how to name latent dimensions. For both STM and EFA, the number of latent dimensions is unknown to the researcher, although the probable number can be hypothesized prior to data analysis. Because of this fundamental uncertainty on that number of topics, multiple models with varying numbers of latent dimensions typically are fitted first, and then the most appropriate number of latent dimensions is decided upon using multiple criteria. Like EFA, STM relies on multiple criteria, and this study employed four indexes: held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, exclusivity, and residuals (see online Supplemental File B, for further description of these criteria).

Given that these diagnostic indexes have limitations since they simply try to maximize the model fit, but do not necessarily improve the substantive interpretation of the model (Chang et al., 2009), the substantive, semantic fit of the preliminary topic models was also examined for coherence through the terms with the highest FRequency and EXclusivity (FREX) scores associated with each topic. FREX scores combine the probability (how frequently the term appears in a topic) and exclusivity (how exclusively the term appears in a topic) of the term within each topic (Bischof & Airoldi, 2012). Different ratios of probability and exclusivity (e.g., 10/90%, 25/75%, 50/50%, 75/25%, 90/10% of probability/exclusivity) were considered because high-frequency terms in one topic also tend to show high frequency in other topics. Independence and relatedness of topics were examined with topic correlations, an important indication of the validity of the topic model. See online Supplemental File B, for further descriptions of preprocessing, diagnostic indexes, FREX scores, and topic correlations.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: The initial document-term matrix (DTM) for this analysis included 28,056 terms consisting of unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams. An n-gram represents a continuous sequence of n numbers of individual words. For example, a bigram is a term consisting of two words such as "African American." Given that STM analysis focuses on frequently used terms to sort categories, to reduce the vocabulary size, those terms with a frequency less than three were removed. The final DTM consisted of 1,298 terms, made up of 1,023 unigrams (e.g., "people," "good"), 262 bigrams (e.g., "Catholic Church," "Jesus Christ"), and 13 trigrams (e.g., "high school friend," "White middle class"). The descriptive statistics of the 1,298 terms are reported in Table 2.

The final DTM consisted of 1,298 terms, made up of 1,023 unigrams (e.g., "people," "good"), 262 bigrams (e.g., "Catholic Church," "Jesus Christ"), and 13 trigrams (e.g., "high school friend," "White middle class"). The descriptive statistics of the 1,298 terms are reported in Table 2.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Because the number of topics is unknown prior to analysis, we fit several topic models with the number of topics varying from two to 10 topics using the four indexes: held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, exclusivity, and residuals. Online Supplemental File B provides Figure S1 with the findings of each index suggesting the best numbers of topics supported by the text data. Most indicators provided support for either two-, three-, or four-topic models. We then compared these three initial models using the topics identified through the top 20 FREX score terms for each topic, the context in which the high FREX score terms were used within the interviews, and the topic distributions across the interviews. Considering these criteria, the three-topic model fitted the interviews best. The top-ranked 20 terms for the three topics are listed in Table 3. After exploring various frequency and exclusivity ratios of each term, 75/25% was the most discriminating among the various combinations. sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Independence or relatedness of topics was examined with topic correlations as an important indication of the validity of the topic model. Topic correlations were calculated using the topic proportions in the interviews. Two correlation methods were used and the coefficients from both correlation methods were very small and nonsignificant, r < .001, indicating that the three topics were independent and discreet. Online Supplemental File B provides details about these two methods.

paragraphs: Although the covariate of spirituality had enough variation unlike religiosity, our topic models with spirituality as a covariate did not produce meaningful results mainly due to the small sample size (N = 16), suggesting that levels of spirituality did not explain the variation of topic proportions across the interviews in a meaningful way. More details can be obtained from the authors. Future studies on a larger sample with enough variability on those covariates are desired.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: The topic model revealed three latent topics or themes that were shared among all of the participants and identified the subgroup of participants who most fully represented each topic. Review of the segments of text associated with each topic and applying an RDS perspective led to our naming the topics Individual, Transcendent, and Social to identify the ecological system most salient to the descriptions of spirituality associated with each topic. In looking at specific language people used, we observed that people derived meaning out of what they perceived to be a source of transcendence (or in some cases of highest value) and this sense of what was meaningful was apparent in their descriptions of their motivations and behaviors. Although ascribing meaning based on transcendent beliefs was common, the participants differed on what ecological system that they referred to when speaking about spirituality. Some emphasized internal reflection processes, some their experiences and/or understanding of transcendence, and others how these experiences or deliberations were relevant to their social relationships.

Topic 1: Individual Topic 1, named Individual, had top-ranked terms and related segments of text that emphasized how participants discussed spirituality at the individual level highlighting reflections and feelings about their beliefs, values, and related behaviors. In general, the key terms (see Table 3) related to the processes of meaning-making (e.g., connection, thinking, beliefs), the content of their meaning system (e.g., the good, nature, religion), or the people with whom they sought meaning (e.g., husband, country). Regardless of their referent of transcendence (e.g., Jesus, the planet, goodness, science, themselves), these participants offered rich descriptions on how spirituality was relevant to their internal thought processes informing their beliefs, values, identity, and what brought meaning in their lives. For example, one participant (56-year-old female, no affiliation, slightly spiritual) described her beliefs about, and feelings for, our planet. These beliefs were evident in her values, her identity as an environmentalist, and the actions she takes to live them out.

Maybe I am spiritual when I think of the planet. I feel for our planet, I feel for it being destroyed … I consider myself an environmentalist. I try to recycle, I don't know if it's doing any GOOD, but I'm doing it anyway. I pick up plastic and trash on the beach every time I walk. I feel that the planet, you know, it's mother earth. You know, maybe that's my God, I consider that my God.
A 29-year-old, very spiritual Buddhist male described how his meditation practices enabled him to live out his beliefs about karma and treating people well.

But yeah, just really trying to have that purity of mind to even when I find myself THINKING about somebody in a negative way, just realize that that's not gonna make anything better. If I don't like the way this PERSON is, I should understand why the PERSON is the way they are, at least. And then that can help me relate to them in a better way. But these are all things that it is very much a PRACTICE, you don't just start doing this. I wish that were possible, but you can't do that.

Another participant (48-year-old female, formally Christian Orthodox, very spiritual) described that she found direction and purpose through spirituality. For her, through being self-aware and being connected to herself, she was able to have a better understanding of her purpose and of what she needed to do:

Spiritual for me is, that CONNECTION to yourself, that CONNECTION to your own soul, to your spirit, to your purpose, to your real you, you know? … . So spirituality for me, brings you back to yourself. It gives you that source of understanding where you have to go, what you need to do. You know, what is the purpose.

paragraphs: Top 20 FREX Score Terms of Topics Topic Terms Individual "good," "connect," "support," "positive," "political," "hope," "country," "person," "follow," "figure," "life," "animal," "husband," "interested," "act," "freedom," "belief," "thinking," "practice," "religion" Transcendence "god," "worship," "Jesus," "home," "assumption," "context," "anchorage," "model," "church," "real," "woman," "baby," "bible," "heaven," "spirituality," "spend," "word," "faith," "child," "black" Social "opinion," "friend," "high school," "school," "group," "dad," "wrestling," "friend friend," "change," "mosque," "middle school," "treat," "thought," "deal," "college," "sense," "parent," "high," "strongly," "middle"
Note. Individual = Topic 1; Transcendent = Topic 2; Social = Topic 3; FREX = FRequency and EXclusivity. Preprocessing before topic modeling analysis converted all terms into lower cases.

Whereas spiritual feels more like connecting with nature, and knowing yourself, and wanting meaningful connection with others in the world and significance in your life. So those sorts of things I think feel different than religion to people. And spiritual is usually how that gets labeled … . Yeah, anything from valuing their family, to liking to be alone in nature, to thinking deeply about philosophical issues, I think could be what different people mean by SPIRITUALITY.

One participant, a 37-year-old, not spiritual, agnostic male, described his commitment to sharing his experience of nature with others. He experienced powerful positive emotions like awe when in nature that prompted questions about transcendence:

When I'm out in that, especially at the RIVER, like my feet in the RIVER, I'm in the RIVER … that's when I say, "Wow. I know that this … this is so beautiful." That's when I start to (ask) … . Is there a higher being that created all of this, or is it all science-based? I don't know. That's where I struggle with that part of it.
A 44-year-old, very spiritual, Protestant female, explained ways she interacts with God, her referent of transcendence, through prayer and reading scripture:

I would love to spend time every day in kind of a focused scripture setting in prayer, like, I'd love to spend half an hour to an hour on that every day … . I'll pray for those things and just kind of sit with GOD and ask him to give me His perspective … kind of talk to GOD and sit quietly and try to listen to GOD kind of prayers.

Topic 3: Social Topic 3, Social, (see Table 3), emphasized specific social nexuses, specifically where (e.g., mosque, middle school, college), with whom (e.g., friend, dad, parent), and how (e.g., wrestling, change, thought, deal, opinion) their spirituality was influenced or expressed. Participants shared formative experiences at home, in congregations, and in communities. In addition, they explained how spirituality informs how they engage people socially.

Topic 3 suggests that spirituality occurs in relational and social systems. For example, a 29-year-old woman with moderate spirituality and no current religious affiliation, referenced the role of a meditation group and her experience growing up in a mixed religious home (e.g., Catholic and Methodist):

About two years ago, my friend was like, "You should come to this meditation GROUP," and I went to that and I don't necessarily buy much into Buddhism, but I do really like the concept of meditation and I like that we'll go out and volunteer and do some helpful things and it's a shared GROUP of people who respect each other and who are ethically similar. So, I think that's what I like about religion.

Participants in this topic shared commonalities of having the latitude to explore, develop, and live their spirituality amongst diverse groups of people whether in their school, extended family, and community.

My DAD primarily raised me. My mom was DEALING with her [expletive] and my DAD was pretty religiously Catholic, very much so Catholic and he never imposed that on me. If I wanted to go to church with my FRIENDS, he would let me go to whatever they did, and so most of them were Non-Denominational. I don't think I ever went to a MOSQUE or anything, but he let me just explore no matter what … .

Regarding this topic, a 23-year-old, slightly spiritual Muslim man reported: I think, in my OPINION, the value of religion as you're growing up, to teach you morals, values, right from wrong, and also put you in a cohesive GROUP where you can socialize with other people and get a little bit of interaction with other people from your community that are not necessarily in your household.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Given changing demographics and the disparate conceptualizations of R/S in the literature, identifying commonalities among diverse beliefs and expressions of spirituality provides challenges. To gain insight into what all people may share in regards to R/S, we explored common psychological tendencies with unique expressions among a vastly diverse sample of adults within the United States. We framed an exploration of nomothetic and idiographic findings through the lens of reciprocating spirituality, which broadly considers the many psychological capacities involved in R/S emphasizing how people make meaning through their experience and response to transcendence. Specifically, we sought to delineate an understanding of spirituality through employing STM analysis to a set of in-depth interviews of spiritually diverse adults, offer an interpretation of these findings based on the integration of two bodies of theoretical work, and illustrate the findings through quotations from the identified topics. We chose STM to minimize bias that occurs with predefined categories and lexicons generated from human judgment. In STM, the statistical model estimates topics based on the pattern of word cooccurrences in the text and assigns interviews to the topics that they predominantly represent. In common language, STM bundles sources of data (in our case interviews) into topics based on how the program sorts words and groups of words based on frequency, proximity, and exclusivity. The topic model allowed for theoretical interpretation of the topics, and the interviews assigned to each topic provided examples of individuals' lived experiences. We used this approach to examine participants' idiosyncratic perspectives around the common themes identified by each topic model.

Our interpretation was guided by our synthesis of RDS and meaning-making approaches to spirituality. An RDS lens supported naming the topics: Individual, Transcendent, and Social reflecting different systems in which spirituality takes place. Meaning-making approaches highlight that persons across the topics spoke about spirituality in terms of how they experienced transcendence, how transcendence inspired meaning in their lives, gave them a sense of connection to others, and provided varying degrees of motivation for behaviors connected to their spirituality. Our study contributes to existing literature by affirming (a) that people of different spiritual inclinations find transcendence in perceptions about realities beyond the material (e.g., supernatural, divine, immanence) or in entities of great importance (e.g., the environment, community); and (b) that transcendence provides meaning which informs values, identity, goals, behaviors, and social interactions. In addition, our findings emphasize an often overlooked aspect of spirituality, (c) that spirituality is experienced and lived out in proximal and distal ecological systems and involves intra, inter, and transpersonal processes.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Topic 1, Individual, highlighted descriptions of internal psychological processes that participants attributed to their spirituality regardless of different ideas about the source of transcendence or This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

immanence, which is consistent with the vast majority of literature that highlights the internal locus of the individual (Harris et al., 2018;King et al., 2021;Pargament et al., 2013;Yaden et al., 2021).

Although participants named many intrapersonal processes through descriptions of self-reflection, self-awareness, experiences meditating, and their motivations, taken together these statements reflected ascription of meaning. Their explicit descriptions of their beliefs, values, goals, identity, and relational and behavioral commitments were often consistent. Further, participants provided evidence that their spiritual beliefs involved transcendent emotions, meaning, and motivated their behaviors associated with spirituality (King et al., 2023;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). For example, one woman spoke of the beauty and importance of trees. She described her deep appreciation of nature with statements about awe and gratitude. Later in the interview, she recounted picking up trash on the beach and her efforts to enlist other people in support of environmental sustainability. Although she did not have a clear sense of how her spirituality operated in her life, her descriptions suggested that her spiritual beliefs invoked transcendent emotions and motivated her to behave accordingly. Other participants' spiritual beliefs were evident in statements about values, identity, and their rationale for generous behaviors such as social justice activism and engaging in like-minded communities with missions toward the greater good. These descriptions are not only consistent with belief and emotion processes described in socioaffective neuroscience on meaning-making (Immordino-Yang, 2016; Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021) but also with research on the relationship between R/S and purpose (Damon, 2009), civic engagement (Steensland et al., 2022), and beyond-the-self contribution (King et al., 2014(King et al., , 2021)). The interviews representing Topic 1 provide examples of how spiritual beliefs and experience enable people to create meaning which can undergird the pursuit of purpose, civic engagement, and prosocial contribution. These descriptions of beliefs, values, goals, identity, and purpose illustrate how people proactively make meaning. These findings extend on Park's (2010) discrepancy-based model of meaning structure which describes how meaning is made in response to adversity and affirm. Immordino-Yang's (2016) research on how young people ascribe meaning that results in prosocial engagement and thriving. Consistent with reciprocating spirituality, these findings provide examples of how individuals find meaning through diverse referents of transcendence and how meaning inspires prosocial action (King & Boyatzis, 2015;King et al., 2014;Lerner et al., 2003). Furthermore, these findings are aligned with positive R/S development, which asserts that people seek coherence through developing cognitive-affective and sociobehavioral patterns that enable the spiritual search for sacred meaning (Davis et al., 2023). Taken together, Topic 1 reinforced meaning-making as a central component to spirituality across belief systems, and the participants' narratives provide examples of how meaning acquired through spiritual pursuits serves to guide and orient people as they continue to make sense of the world and understand their roles in it.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Participants in Topic 2 provided descriptions emphasizing beliefs about and/or experiences of transcendence across different belief systems. The participants' descriptions of transcendence were consistent with the literature, unified around a referent (i.e., God, math, and/or nature). Whether theistic, supernatural, and/or otherwise, the transcendent referents were perceived to have qualities such as boundlessness and ultimacy (Pargament et al., 2013) and provided sources of meaning and significance (Park, 2010) informing participants' identity and motivating actions (King et al., 2014) toward something not only beyond, but better than themselves (Harris et al., 2018).

Although beliefs about transcendence are generally considered macrosystemic and transcendence is often conceptualized as something "beyond the self," four of the five participants representing Topic 2, described a high level of intimacy or at times communion with their understanding of transcendence. For example, they articulated feeling "one with nature" or "close to God," corresponding to existing research that details how transcendence can be experienced as a profound cognitive and affective connection (King et al., 2014;Yaden et al., 2021). This connection may be experienced with God relationally (see Davis et al., 2023;Sandage et al., 2020), as an attachment figure (Granqvist & Shaver, 2020), or in other cases with sources of ultimate reality that are luminous enough to provide orienting power to life (Pargament et al., 2013). Whether their transcendent experiences occurred in ordinary (e.g., experiencing awe in nature) or extraordinary (e.g., noticing "signs" from loved ones who have died) circumstances, these experiences held great meaning, and inspired people to continue to engage their spirituality. The participants' recounted how reading scripture, reflecting on math, and excursions in nature deepened their connection to their referent of transcendence. This finding corresponds to research on relational spirituality (Sandage et al., 2020), reciprocating spirituality (King & Boyatzis, 2015;King et al., 2014), and positive R/S development (Davis et al., 2023) which posit that spiritual practices can deepen engagement with transcendence.

In addition, Topic 2 participants articulated how their behaviors were influenced by their experience and understanding of transcendence. For example, they intentionally shared what they held as transcendent with others (e.g., organizing excursions in nature or providing spiritual direction) and were motivated to act on their convictions (e.g., doing social justice work in their community, teaching in schools or church). Whereas Topic 1 participants' descriptions emphasized personal beliefs and reflection on them, Topic 2 participants elaborated more about their experience of transcendence-how connected they felt and how their life orientation and commitments were informed by their profound connections to the transcendent. To a greater extent than in Topic 1, when speaking about their referent of transcendence, Topic 2 participants described transcendent emotions like awe, gratitude, and elevation and also articulated a sense of commitment, which was evident in their actions. As noted above, these narratives align with the meaning-making processes demonstrated in current research explaining the importance of transcendent thoughts and feelings for the integration of beliefs into narrative identity that has enduring influence on relational and behavioral commitments (Immordino-Yang, 2016;King et al., 2014;Riveros & Immordino-Yang, 2021). Topic 2 participants illustrated the idea of reciprocating spirituality with their mental activities involving awareness and connection to their referents. Moreover, they exhibited values-aligned behaviors that served to further their engagement with God, nature, or math (Balswick et al., 2016;King & Boyatzis, 2015). Similarly, positive This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.
R/S development emphasizes the reciprocal nature of internal cognitive-affective R/S habits (i.e., reflection) and enacted sociobehavioral R/S habits (Davis et al., 2023).

Topic 3: Social Social context was the common theme of Topic 3. Participants' descriptions of spirituality highlighted different microsystems for the development and maintenance of spirituality, emphasizing both people and places that were formative. Consistent with existing research these participants identified family members and friends with whom they shared spiritual beliefs and practices or who influenced their spiritual development (Dollahite & Marks, 2018;Mahoney, 2010;Mahoney et al., 2005). Similarly, participants named their homes, communities, and religious congregations as being salient spaces in which they pursued their spirituality (Dollahite & Marks, 2018;King et al., 2014). Participants described how various communities (e.g., school, meditation group, Air Force) were formative and described how they were raised in environments that encouraged exploration outside of their families' affiliations.

In addition, some of the participants associated with Topic 3 described how their spirituality guided their interactions with others. For example, spiritual principles prompted openness to other people's perspectives and opinions. This finding affirms that although spirituality often involves abstract beliefs regarding ultimate realities, spirituality is also lived out in the context of common relationships. Spirituality is not only influenced and shaped by people and communities, but spirituality informs relational ethics influencing how individuals treat and engage others (King et al., 2020;Mahoney, 2010). Sharing with others appeared to bolster the participants' own beliefs and helped them in creating and maintaining relationships. Topic 3 participants' narratives underscored that relational spirituality not only involves relating to a referent of transcendence, but that spirituality is an important element of interpersonal relationships (Sandage et al., 2020).

Participants in Topic 2 provided descriptions emphasizing beliefs about and/or experiences of transcendence across different belief systems. The participants' descriptions of transcendence were consistent with the literature, unified around a referent (i.e., God, math, and/or nature). Whether theistic, supernatural, and/or otherwise, the transcendent referents were perceived to have qualities such as boundlessness and ultimacy (Pargament et al., 2013) and provided sources of meaning and significance (Park, 2010) informing participants' identity and motivating actions (King et al., 2014) toward something not only beyond, but better than themselves (Harris et al., 2018).

Although beliefs about transcendence are generally considered macrosystemic and transcendence is often conceptualized as something "beyond the self," four of the five participants representing Topic 2, described a high level of intimacy or at times communion with their understanding of transcendence. For example, they articulated feeling "one with nature" or "close to God," corresponding to existing research that details how transcendence can be experienced as a profound cognitive and affective connection (King et al., 2014;Yaden et al., 2021). This connection may be experienced with God relationally (see Davis et al., 2023;Sandage et al., 2020), as an attachment figure (Granqvist & Shaver, 2020), or in other cases with sources of ultimate reality that are luminous enough to provide orienting power to life (Pargament et al., 2013). Whether their transcendent experiences occurred in ordinary (e.g., experiencing awe in nature) or extraordinary (e.g., noticing "signs" from loved ones who have died) circumstances, these experiences held great meaning, and inspired people to continue to engage their spirituality. The participants' recounted how reading scripture, reflecting on math, and excursions in nature deepened their connection to their referent of transcendence. This finding corresponds to research on relational spirituality (Sandage et al., 2020), reciprocating spirituality (King & Boyatzis, 2015;King et al., 2014), and positive R/S development (Davis et al., 2023) which posit that spiritual practices can deepen engagement with transcendence.

In addition, Topic 2 participants articulated how their behaviors were influenced by their experience and understanding of transcendence. For example, they intentionally shared what they held as transcendent with others (e.g., organizing excursions in nature or providing spiritual direction) and were motivated to act on their convictions (e.g., doing social justice work in their community, teaching in schools or church). Whereas Topic 1 participants' descriptions emphasized personal beliefs and reflection on them, Topic 2 participants elaborated more about their experience of transcendence-how connected they felt and how their life orientation and commitments were informed by their profound connections to the transcendent. sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: We note that STM was applied to identify the most salient latent themes in the interviews, lowering the bias toward any particular framework. Rather than the ecological framework we found, we anticipated potential topics to congeal around differences between religiousness and spirituality, different referents of transcendence, various psychological processes engaged in spirituality (e.g., beliefs about transcendence, meaning-making), or distinctions between spiritual attitudes and behaviors. Despite being rooted in an RDS meta-perspective, we did not anticipate topics related to different systems.

Because STM is a mixed-membership topic model in which pieces of text data can belong to multiple latent classes at the same time (Airoldi et al., 2014), our findings convey that the theme of meaning-making and related psychological processes highlighted in Topic 1 was evident across all interviews. Throughout the other topics, participants described how their spiritual beliefs were related to what was salient in their worldviews, values, identity, goals, purposes, and relationships. Similarly, the theme of Topic 2, Transcendence, was evident in Topics 1 and 3. Although participants in Topic 1 emphasized their spirituality in mental activities, they also named the sources of their meaning, often perceived to be transcendent or immanent sources of truth. That said, we note that in Topic 1, some of the participants' descriptions of spirituality did not involve referents that would be characterized as beyond the material. Rather, they named sources of meaning that were important to them like trees or a community that informed their values and priorities. However, all Topic 3 participants identified an immaterial source of transcendence, but their interviews emphasized spirituality being experienced in relationships. Likewise, social themes of Topic 3 were apparent across all topics. For example, participants associated with Topic 1 named people with whom they shared common values or pursued their spiritual goals. Those in Topic 2 identified people who were involved in spiritual experiences, such as a woman who felt the presence of her late father. In addition, for all topics, places such as work, worship services, or particular locations were mentioned in their descriptions of spirituality.

In summary, each topic reflected many of the cognitive (i.e., R/S beliefs, reflection), affective (i.e., transcendent emotions, motivation), and behavioral (i.e., prayer, service) aspects of R/S that are common in the literature discussed in this article and beyond (see Pargament et al., 2013;Saroglou, 2011). In spite of the topic, the recurrence of themes pertaining to transcendence inspiring meaning, resulting in fidelity to beliefs and worldviews, and motivating valued-aligned behaviors suggests that meaning-making through transcendence is a viable and important frame for understanding diverse forms of spirituality. We also note that the behaviors associated with spirituality across the topics were generally intended to benefit others or the environment. Exceptions included activities or practices that enabled the individual to experience spirituality (e.g., meditation, excursions in nature). Although our findings provide examples of how psychological capacities are engaged in underrepresented forms of spirituality, given the small sample, we are hesitant to generalize. Although we commend this integration of RDS and meaning-making as a frame for understanding spirituality, the significance of the contribution of our findings is both in what psychological capacities comprise R/S, and where these psychological capacities are engaged in the pursuit and experience of R/S. Spirituality is all too often studied as an individual construct, with less attention given to the contexts in which spirituality occurs and to studying the complex and reciprocal influences between individual, social, and transcendent levels.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
sections.header_raw: Limitations and Directions for Future Research
sections.header_norm: limitations and directions for future research
paragraphs: Our study had many strengths. Most pointedly, we framed the study with an integrative theoretical framework, and our methods involved in-depth narratives from individuals with diverse beliefs. The study provided examples of lived experience, and we used a rigorous analytic methodology. The interdisciplinary nature of our research team (e.g., first author: clinical psychologist, second author: family social scientist, third author: developmental psychologist) was also a strength. However, there are several important limitations to note. First, this study conducted a secondary data analysis. Although our research questions were aligned with Fetzer's original aims, we did not design the questionnaire, which may have allowed for the creators' and reviewers' biases. Second, STM as an analytic method This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

aimed to reduce bias that would emerge from conventional qualitative data analysis (e.g., content analysis); however, a second round of human-centered interpretation was required to name the topics. Third, the data set was limited to 16 interviews and generalizability is limited in spite of the rich descriptions of spirituality offered.

Although the level of spirituality (low vs. high) was used as a covariate to predict the topic proportions, the sample size (N = 16) was not large enough to reliably predict its relationship to topic proportions. In addition, the data set for this study was limited to adults living in the United States in 2018 and does not consider cultural or geographic diversity beyond the United States. Although the sample represented different perspectives on spirituality, religious perspectives were limited. Only two participants in the sample identified as moderately or highly religious, and all but one of the others reported being a part of a religious tradition at some point in time during their lives. Although these findings represent the analysis on people's views of R/S generally, perspectives about religiousness could not be adequately evaluated. Further research is warranted to explore how levels of spirituality, religiousness, and other demographics such as gender, race, and age may predict topic proportions in the interviews.

The findings of this study point to ways people find meaning, connection, and inspiration through spirituality. Interesting to note is how individuals varied in how and in what they found meaning or created meaning systems. Some individuals had more cohesive or comprehensive meaning systems, while others appeared to be searching. A possibility to further investigate is the question of whether the extent of perceived transcendence (i.e., more ultimate, more luminous, more sacred) makes a difference to the coherence of the meaning system and the extent of its influence on one's values, identity, behaviors, and relationships.

Additionally, longitudinal research is warranted to track and understand intraindividual changes involved with spiritual development over time. The findings of this study reveal a myriad of individual, social, and transcendent level influences that potentially contribute to nonlinear changes in spirituality that should be considered in future research (King et al., 2021). Specifically, the findings point to the importance of considering the many psychological capacities involved in making meaning, apprehending transcendence, and relating to different types of people as central to spirituality. We identify the complexity of how further studies will need to consider the relationships between cognitive, effective, and behavioral dimensions of human functioning in order to further delineate how people find meaning, inspiration, connection through spirituality. Furthermore, the findings of this study highlight that people consider their actions and behaviors as part of spirituality. These findings align with reciprocating spirituality and positive R/S development, which both emphasize that spirituality involves cognitive, affective, social, and behavioral capacities, whereas much of the literature focuses on internal cognitive and affective processes and considers behaviors like prosocial actions or civic engagement as outcomes (see Hardy et al., 2019;Steensland et al., 2022).

Although further research is warranted to understand how this ecological perspective informs R/S, initial implications suggest that practitioners-whether spiritual leaders, clergy, or clinicians-should consider a variety of channels for nurturing spirituality. Practitioners need to be aware of various referents of transcendence beyond conventional religious understandings that may serve as sources of meaning and inspiration. In addition, practitioners may support the development of psychological capacities such as self-awareness and emotion regulation that enable people to derive meaning, purpose, and belonging through their spiritual practices, in which they connect to the transcendent and through their connections to others.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Spirituality and meaning-making across contexts Structural topic modeling of the Fetzer spiritualit
paragraphs: Our study identifies both unifying and contrasting expressions of spirituality in the USA in the early part of the 21st century, as well as offers vivid descriptions from diverse lived experiences. Analysis based on STM resulted in three discrete topics that were explained by RDS and identified through the mental processes within the individual, between people at a social level, and through peoples' perceptions of transcendence. Consistent with the literature, the narrative interviews provided descriptions of spirituality that convey how people with diverse beliefs find and make meaning that informs and inspires how they live out their lives for the greater good. This study affirms transcendent meaning-making as a potential frame for further research on spirituality and provides insight into how spirituality is lived out within and among persons and their perceptions of the transcendent. As text data analysis methodologies grow in their complexity and as we further delineate the psychological and ecological factors involved in R/S development, future research will be able to shed more light on nomothetic and idiosyncratic patterns of spiritual development that lead to human thriving and flourishing societies. 1
sections.text: Our study identifies both unifying and contrasting expressions of spirituality in the USA in the early part of the 21st century, as well as offers vivid descriptions from diverse lived experiences. Analysis based on STM resulted in three discrete topics that were explained by RDS and identified through the mental processes within the individual, between people at a social level, and through peoples' perceptions of transcendence. Consistent with the literature, the narrative interviews provided descriptions of spirituality that convey how people with diverse beliefs find and make meaning that informs and inspires how they live out their lives for the greater good. This study affirms transcendent meaning-making as a potential frame for further research on spirituality and provides insight into how spirituality is lived out within and among persons and their perceptions of the transcendent. As text data analysis methodologies grow in their complexity and as we further delineate the psychological and ecological factors involved in R/S development, future research will be able to shed more light on nomothetic and idiosyncratic patterns of spiritual development that lead to human thriving and flourishing societies. 1
